food_name: Steamed Fish Head with Chopped Chilies
aliases: Steamed Fish Head with Chopped Chilies

english: Steamed Fish Head with Chopped Chilies

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn entry exists specifically for Duo Jiao Yu Tou. Closest defensible match is the generic steamed fish food product class (FOODON_03302051). Exact Hunan-style preparation with fermented chili topping is not represented.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKP (Fish, freshwater, cooked) is the closest defensible anchor for bighead carp head, steamed. The fermented chili condiment layer is not captured in FoodEx2 at this level of specificity.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT food concept found for this specific Hunan dish preparation.

preparation_method: topped with fermented chopped red chilies

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Steamed Fish Head with Chopped Chilies is one whole fish head (approximately 400g edible portion), shared among 2–4 diners as a centrepiece main dish.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Bighead carp, cooked (closest approved analogue); supplemented with USDA SR Legacy data for fermented chili condiment contribution
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Duo Jiao Yu Tou exists in any approved primary source. Values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked freshwater carp (bighead carp, steamed) combined with estimated contributions from fermented red chili paste (duo jiao), scallions, ginger, and cooking oil. Sodium is elevated significantly due to the fermented chili and soy sauce components. The input AI-generated values (confidence 0.9) are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate and have been retained with minor cross-checks. Detailed fatty acid fractions (monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, trans fat), cholesterol, and water content are set to -1 as these are not reliably disaggregated for this composite dish in any approved source.

gi_source.title: Analogous estimate — low-carbohydrate fish dish with chili and aromatics; University of Sydney GI Database consulted
gi_source.source_match_notes: Steamed Fish Head with Chopped Chilies contains only 1.5g carbohydrate per 100g, almost entirely from the chili and aromatics. The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and contains no direct entry for this dish. A GI of 15 (low) is assigned as an analogous estimate based on the negligible carbohydrate content and the established low-GI classification of high-protein, high-fat fish dishes with minimal starch. Glycemic load per 400g serving is estimated at approximately 0.9 (1.5g carb per 100g × 4 × GI 15 / 100), which is clinically negligible.

hypertension.key_factor: Very high sodium content (450mg per 100g; approximately 1800mg per 400g serving) from fermented chopped chilies and soy sauce
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit or avoid this dish due to its very high sodium load; a single 400g serving provides approximately 1800mg sodium, approaching or exceeding the WHO daily recommended limit of 2000mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Negligible carbohydrate content (1.5g per 100g) and high protein from fish; minimal glycemic impact
type2_diabetes.guidance: This dish is suitable for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes as it is very low in carbohydrates and high in lean protein, resulting in negligible postprandial blood glucose elevation; however, sodium intake should still be monitored.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium content is a cardiovascular risk factor; fish provides beneficial omega-3 fatty acids that may offset some risk
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The high sodium content (approximately 1800mg per serving) is a significant concern for cardiovascular disease management, though the fish head provides omega-3 fatty acids with cardioprotective properties; portion control and reduced-sodium preparation are recommended.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Fish provides omega-3 fatty acids; capsaicin in chilies has anti-inflammatory properties
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to omega-3 content and anti-inflammatory properties of capsaicin and ginger; note that nightshade vegetables (chilies) may trigger symptoms in a subset of individuals.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Steamed Fish Head with Chopped Chilies (剁椒鱼头, Duo Jiao Yu Tou) is the signature dish of Hunan (Xiang) cuisine in China. It is prepared by splitting a large, fresh bighead carp head in half and laying it flat on a steaming plate, then covering it generously with 'duo jiao' — a traditional Hunanese condiment of fermented, salted, and chopped red chilies. The dish is steamed over high heat for approximately 15–20 minutes until the fish flesh is tender and gelatinous. It is finished by scattering fresh scallions and ginger over the top and pouring a ladle of smoking-hot oil over them, which releases a powerful, fragrant aroma. The flavour profile is intensely spicy, salty, and slightly sour from the fermentation, balanced by the natural sweetness and richness of the fish head meat. The texture is prized for its succulence and the collagen-rich, gelatinous quality of the cheeks, lips, and head meat. Nutritionally, the dish is high in protein (14.5g per 100g), moderate in fat (5.2g per 100g), and very low in carbohydrates (1.5g per 100g), making it suitable for low-carbohydrate and diabetic dietary patterns. However, the sodium content is very high (450mg per 100g; ~1800mg per 400g serving) due to the fermented chili and soy sauce, making it unsuitable for individuals on sodium-restricted diets. The dish is a celebratory centrepiece in Hunan banquets and is widely available across China and in Chinese restaurants globally.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Duo Jiao Yu Tou exists in any approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia MyFCD, or FAO/INFOODS SEA). Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FoodData Central data for cooked freshwater carp (bighead carp, steamed) with estimated contributions from fermented chili, soy sauce, scallions, ginger, and cooking oil. The input AI-generated values are broadly consistent with this USDA-anchored estimate. Medium confidence is appropriate given the composite nature of the dish and absence of a direct approved-source match.
notes: 1. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FoodData Central (cooked freshwater carp, steamed) as the closest approved-source analogue; no exact entry for Duo Jiao Yu Tou exists in any approved primary source. Sodium is significantly elevated due to fermented chili (duo jiao) and soy sauce components. 2. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, cholesterol_mg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractions, cholesterol, and moisture data for this composite Hunan dish are not available in USDA FoodData Central or any other approved source as of 2026-04-16. 3. glycemic_index is set to 15 (low) as an analogous estimate; this dish contains only 1.5g carbohydrate per 100g and has negligible glycemic impact. Glycemic load per 400g serving is estimated at approximately 0.9 (clinically negligible). The University of Sydney GI Database was consulted and contains no direct entry for this dish. 4. The input source 'Hunan Academy of Agricultural Sciences' (http://www.hnaas.cn/) is not on the approved source list and has not been used as primary evidence. 5. dietary_tags in the input incorrectly listed 'vegetarian' and 'vegan'; these have been corrected to false as the dish contains fish. 6. Duplicate portion entry in input has been deduplicated to a single 400g serving. 7. saturated_fat_g estimated at 1.3g per 100g based on USDA data for cooked carp fat composition (approximately 25% of total fat as saturated).
